2: ûḇᵉal-bāʾ ʾel-hêkal YHWH — 'but he did not enter the temple of YHWH' (2 Chronicles 27:2). The clause is the Chronicler's key characterization of Jotham's reign: he did what was right in YHWH's eyes, but unlike his father Uzziah he did not transgress the sanctuary boundary. The negative specification — restraint, not innovation — is presented as virtue. The Chronicler records the shortest account of a 'good' king in Chronicles here (9 verses), suggesting that absence of transgression is a positive category in the covenantal ledger. The phrase wayyᵉšaḥēt hāʿām ʿôḏ (the people still acted corruptly) forms a foil: the king's personal piety could not prevent communal corruption.
6: wayyithazzēq yôtām kî hēkîn dᵉrākāyw — 'Jotham became mighty because he ordered his ways before YHWH his God' (2 Chronicles 27:6). The hithazzēq (Hithpael of ḥāzaq, reflexive — strengthened himself) echoes the wayyitkazzēq of Solomon in 2 Chronicles 1:1, the charge in 1 Chronicles 22:13, and David's repeated use of the same verb. The Chronicler uses this as a theological seal-word: a king who 'orders his ways' (hēkîn dᵉrākāyw) achieves the strengthening that is divine gift filtered through covenantal fidelity. The kî causal conjunction makes the covenant logic explicit — strength is the result of ordered-before-YHWH living, not independent achievement.
1: yôṯām ben-ʿuzziyyāhû — 'Jotham son of Uzziah' (2 Chronicles 27:1). The accession formula for Jotham is brief: twenty-five years old, sixteen-year reign, mother Jerushah daughter of Zadok. The Zadokite maternal lineage is notable: Jerushah's father Zadok is likely a Levitical/priestly family connection. Jotham's reign (c. 750-735 BC) overlaps with the ministry of Isaiah, Hosea, and Micah — the major eighth-century prophets. The Chronicler's brief chapter (9 verses) reflects the brevity of 2 Kings 15:32-38, which also gives Jotham little coverage despite his positive evaluation.
3: hûʾ bānāh ʾet-šáʿar bêt-YHWH hāʿelyôn ûḇəḥômat hāʿōpel bānāh lārōḇ — 'He built the Upper Gate of YHWH's house and did extensive work on the Ophel wall' (2 Chronicles 27:3). šáʿar bêt-YHWH hāʿelyôn (Upper Gate of YHWH's house) — the north gate of the inner court, between the temple courtyard and the outer court (cf. Ezekiel 9:2; 2 Kings 15:35). The Ophel (hāʿōpel) is the hill between the city of David and the temple mount — Jotham extended the temple-hill fortification. His first act as king is temple construction: the Chronicler's highest praise-indicator.
4: wəʿārîm bānāh bəhar yəhûḏāh ûḇehārîm bānāh bîrāniyyôt ûmiggdālîm — 'He also built towns in the hill country of Judah and constructed fortresses and towers in the forests' (2 Chronicles 27:4). The hill-country building program — towns, fortresses (bîrāniyyôt, citadels), and towers (miggdālîm) in forested areas — extends Uzziah's defensive network (2 Chronicles 26:9-10) into Judah's interior. The Chronicler's brief notice mirrors the format used for Rehoboam's fortified cities (2 Chronicles 11:5-12). Physical infrastructure correlated with spiritual fidelity is the Chronicler's consistent pattern.
5: wəhûʾ nilḥam ʿim-melek bənê-ʿammôn wayyiḵbal ʿălêhem ûbənê ʿammôn nāṯnû-lô baššānāh hahîʾ mēʾāh kikkār-kāsep wəʿăśeret ʾălāpîm kōrîm ḥiṭṭîm ûʿăśārāh ʾălāpîm śəʿōrîm — 'He fought against the king of the Ammonites, defeated them, and the Ammonites paid him that year one hundred talents of silver, ten thousand cors of wheat, and ten thousand cors of barley' (2 Chronicles 27:5). The Ammonite tribute (silver + grain in kind) matches the format of Uzziah's Ammonite tribute (2 Chronicles 26:8). The repetition of tribute from Ammon under both father and son marks it as a stable vassal relationship under Judah's military strength. The grain quantities — 10,000 cors of each cereal — are substantial (a cor = approximately 200 liters).
7: wəyeter diḇrê yôṯām wəkol-milḥāmōṯāyw wədarākāyw hinnām kəṯûḇîm ʿal-sēper malḵê yiśrāʾēl wîhûḏāh — 'The rest of the acts of Jotham and all his wars and his ways are recorded in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah' (2 Chronicles 27:7). The archival citation includes both 'wars' (milḥāmōṯāyw) and 'ways' (darākāyw) — the military and moral record together. The Chronicler's dual reference implies a more complete account available to his audience, with only the theologically significant elements selected for Chronicles. The phrase 'Israel and Judah' treats the two kingdoms as a single archival source.
8: ben-ʿeśrîm wəḥāmēš šānāh hāyāh bəmalekô ûšeš ʿeśrēh šānāh mālaḵ bîrûšālaim — 'He was twenty-five years old when he became king and reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem' (2 Chronicles 27:8). The accession formula is repeated at the close of the chapter — unusual in Chronicles, where it typically appears only at the opening. The repetition here (vv1 and 8 both give age 25, 16 years) likely reflects a textual tradition that preserved both the opening and closing summaries as parallel witnesses. Sixteen years ending around 735 BC places Jotham's death near the beginning of the Syro-Ephraimite war (2 Kings 15:37).
9: wayyiškaḇ yôṯām ʿim-ʾăḇōṯāyw wayyiqbəruhû bəʿîr dāwiḏ wayyimlek ʾāḥāz bənô taḥtāyw — 'Jotham slept with his ancestors and was buried in the city of David. His son Ahaz became king in his place' (2 Chronicles 27:9). The standard regnal close carries a special weight here: Jotham's burial 'in the city of David' — the Davidic royal tombs — is granted fully, without qualification. The next sentence, 'Ahaz his son became king,' is the darkest transition in this section: from Judah's last consistently faithful southern king to the most apostate king before Manasseh. The structural contrast is the Chronicler's purpose in keeping 2 Chronicles 27 brief: it is the hinge between faithfulness (26-27) and failure (28).
